section.title: The Anointing and the Last Supper
section.sectionRationale: The Passion narrative opens. As the leaders plot and Judas agrees to betray Him, a woman lavishly anoints Jesus for His burial, a beautiful deed set against the ugliness of betrayal. At the Passover, Jesus reinterprets the bread and cup as His body given and His blood of the covenant poured out for many, instituting the Lord's Supper. He then foretells that all will fall away and Peter will deny Him three times.
section.thesis: On the eve of His death Jesus is honoured by a woman's extravagant devotion and betrayed by a friend, and He transforms the Passover into the sign of His own sacrifice, His body given and His covenant blood poured out for many, even as He foretells the failure of all His disciples.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the plot and the anointing at Bethany; Judas's agreement to betray; the preparation of the Passover; the institution of the Lord's Supper; and the prediction that all will fall away and Peter will deny Him.

units.label: The plot and the anointing at Bethany
units.note: a beautiful deed for My burial

verses.text: Now the Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread were two days away, and the chief priests and scribes were looking for a covert way to arrest Jesus and kill Him.
“But not during the feast,” they said, “or there may be a riot among the people.”

While Jesus was in Bethany reclining at the table in the home of Simon the Leper, a woman came with an alabaster jar of expensive perfume, made of pure nard. She broke open the jar and poured it on Jesus’ head.
Some of those present, however, expressed their indignation to one another: “Why this waste of perfume?
It could have been sold for over three hundred denarii and the money given to the poor.” And they scolded her.
But Jesus said, “Leave her alone; why are you bothering her? She has done a beautiful deed to Me.
The poor you will always have with you, and you can help them whenever you want. But you will not always have Me.
She has done what she could to anoint My body in advance of My burial.
And truly I tell you, wherever the gospel is preached in all the world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her.”

units.label: Judas agrees to betray
units.note: one of the Twelve went to the chief priests

verses.text: Then Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, went to the chief priests to betray Jesus to them.
They were delighted to hear this, and they promised to give him money. So Judas began to look for an opportunity to betray Jesus.

units.label: Preparing the Passover
units.note: a large upper room, furnished and ready

verses.text: On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread, when the {{pascha|Passover}} lamb was to be sacrificed, Jesus’ disciples asked Him, “Where do You want us to prepare for You to eat the Passover?”
So He sent two of His disciples and told them, “Go into the city, and a man carrying a jug of water will meet you. Follow him,
and whichever house he enters, say to the owner, ‘The Teacher asks: Where is My guest room, where I may eat the Passover with My disciples?’
And he will show you a large upper room, furnished and ready. Make preparations for us there.”
So the disciples left and went into the city, where they found everything as Jesus had described. And they prepared the Passover.

units.label: The Last Supper
units.note: this is My body, this is My blood of the covenant

verses.text: When evening came, Jesus arrived with the Twelve.
And while they were reclining and eating, Jesus said, “Truly I tell you, one of you who is eating with Me will betray Me.”
They began to be grieved and to ask Him one after another, “Surely not I?”
He answered, “It is one of the Twelve, the one who is dipping his hand into the bowl with Me.
The Son of Man will go just as it is written about Him, but woe to that man by whom He is betrayed! It would be better for him if he had not been born.”
While they were eating, Jesus took bread, spoke a blessing and broke it, and gave it to the disciples, saying, “Take it; this is My {{soma|body}}.”
Then He took the cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, and they all drank from it.
He said to them, “This is My {{haima|blood}} of the {{diatheke|covenant}}, which is poured out for many.
Truly I tell you, I will no longer drink of the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it anew in the kingdom of God.”
And when they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives.
Then Jesus said to them, “You will all fall away, for it is written: ‘I will strike the Shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered.’
But after I have risen, I will go ahead of you into Galilee.”
Peter declared, “Even if all fall away, I never will.”
“Truly I tell you,” Jesus replied, “this very night, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny Me three times.”
But Peter kept insisting, “Even if I have to die with You, I will never deny You.” And all the others said the same thing.

pascha.gloss: Passover, from the Aramaic and Hebrew for the feast commemorating Israel's rescue from Egypt, when the blood of the lamb spared the firstborn. Jesus deliberately sets His death at Passover and reinterprets the meal around Himself: He is the true Passover lamb whose blood delivers His people.

soma.gloss: Body. Taking bread, Jesus says, this is My body, giving His disciples a sign of His life offered up for them. The broken bread points to His body given in death, and the Supper becomes the ongoing memorial and means of sharing in that sacrifice.

haima.gloss: Blood. Jesus calls the cup His blood poured out for many, evoking the blood of the Passover lamb and the blood that sealed the covenant at Sinai. His death is a sacrificial, life-giving offering that inaugurates the new covenant promised by the prophets.

diatheke.gloss: Covenant, a binding relationship God establishes. Jesus' blood is the blood of the covenant, echoing Exodus 24 and the new covenant of Jeremiah 31. By His death He seals a new covenant in which sins are forgiven and God's people are bound to Him forever.

basic.context: As Passover approaches, the leaders plot to kill Jesus quietly. At a home in Bethany, a woman pours a jar of costly perfume over Jesus' head. Some scold her for the waste, but Jesus defends her: she has done a beautiful thing, anointing Him beforehand for burial, and her deed will be told wherever the gospel goes. Judas, one of the Twelve, then agrees to betray Jesus for money. Jesus prepares to eat the Passover in a borrowed upper room. At the meal He announces that one of them will betray Him, then takes bread and cup and gives them new meaning: this is My body, this is My blood of the covenant, poured out for many. He institutes the Lord's Supper. On the way out, He foretells that all will fall away and that Peter will deny Him three times before the rooster crows twice.
basic.hermeneutics: Mark frames the anointing between the plot and Judas's betrayal, so the woman's devotion shines against the surrounding treachery. The Last Supper is the interpretive centre: Jesus tells us in advance what His death means, a sacrifice, a Passover, a covenant sealed in blood for the forgiveness of many. The predictions of desertion and denial show that even the closest disciples are sinners He must die to save.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus sets His death at the pascha, the Passover. He gives bread as His soma, body, and the cup as His haima, blood, of the diatheke, covenant, poured out for many.
basic.typology: Jesus is the true Passover lamb, His blood delivering His people as the lamb's blood did in Egypt. His blood of the covenant fulfils the covenant blood at Sinai and the new covenant of Jeremiah. The struck Shepherd fulfils Zechariah's prophecy.
basic.christ: Jesus is the anointed one prepared for burial, the true Passover lamb, and the covenant-maker whose body is given and blood poured out for many. He goes to death knowingly, in fulfilment of Scripture.
basic.god: God's plan of salvation unfolds exactly as written; even the betrayal serves it, without excusing the betrayer. God establishes a new covenant through the blood of His Son, for the forgiveness of many.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 gives the Passover lamb. Exodus 24 and Jeremiah 31 give the covenant blood and the new covenant. Zechariah 13 foretells the struck Shepherd.
basic.oneStory: The Passover that rescued Israel from Egypt finds its fulfilment as Jesus, the true Lamb, prepares to give His body and blood for many. In the upper room He seals the new covenant promised long before, in which sins are forgiven forever. The devotion of one woman and the failure of all the disciples frame the meal, showing that the sacrifice He is about to make is for sinners like them, and like us.

context.literary: Chapter 14 begins Mark's Passion narrative. The anointing at Bethany is sandwiched between the leaders' plot, verses 1 to 2, and Judas's betrayal, verses 10 to 11, contrasting extravagant love with treachery. The Last Supper stands at the heart, interpreting the death to come, and is followed by the predictions of desertion and denial that set up the events in Gethsemane and the courtyard.
context.historical: Passover commemorated the exodus, with the lamb slain and eaten in family groups in Jerusalem. Pure nard was an imported perfume; three hundred denarii was nearly a year's wages, so the anointing was extraordinarily costly. Reclining at table was the festive posture. Betraying a rabbi to the authorities during the crowded, tense feast required an inside opportunity, which Judas supplied.
context.canonical: Jesus' words over the cup echo Exodus 24:8, the blood of the covenant Moses sprinkled at Sinai, and Jeremiah 31:31-34, the promised new covenant with sins forgiven. He is the Passover lamb of Exodus 12, as Paul says in 1 Corinthians 5:7. The struck Shepherd and scattered sheep fulfil Zechariah 13:7, and the Supper is handed on as church practice in 1 Corinthians 11:23-26.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interpret Jesus' approaching death before it happens, as the true Passover sacrifice and the sealing of a new covenant in His blood for many, and to frame it with the contrast of devoted love and betrayal, and with the sobering truth that all the disciples will fail, so that the reader sees the cross as the saving purpose of God for sinners.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes unique, once-for-all events. The institution of the Lord's Supper is prescriptive for the church, which continues to eat the bread and drink the cup in remembrance of Christ. The woman's anointing is commended as an example of wholehearted devotion, though not a rule about perfume; the predictions of failure warn against self-confidence.

debates.question: In what sense is the bread Jesus' body and the cup His blood?

views.label: A covenant sign that truly conveys what it signifies
views.case: Jesus, bodily present and not yet crucified, gives bread and wine as signs of His self-giving; the Supper is no empty symbol but a God-given means by which believers really share in the benefits of His sacrifice by faith.

views.label: A memorial that proclaims His death
views.case: The Supper is a remembrance that vividly sets forth the body given and blood shed, uniting the church to that once-for-all sacrifice as they proclaim the Lord's death until He comes, without repeating it.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the Supper points to and applies the one sacrifice of Christ; it is a covenant meal that both remembers His death and feeds faith on its saving benefits.

debates.question: How can Jesus' betrayal be both foreordained and Judas's guilt real?

views.label: God's sovereign plan and human responsibility together
views.case: The Son of Man goes as it is written, yet woe to the betrayer; Scripture holds both, so that God's saving purpose is accomplished through, but is not excused by, Judas's freely chosen sin.

views.label: A warning against presuming on nearness to Jesus
views.case: That a member of the Twelve, who dipped in the same bowl, could betray Him warns every disciple that proximity and privilege are no guarantee; the heart must be truly Christ's, not merely near Him.

debates.bearing: Both hold that divine sovereignty does not cancel human guilt, and that closeness to Jesus without a true heart is a grave and real danger.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus deliberately anchors His death in the pascha, the Passover, the feast of redemption by the blood of the lamb. At the table He takes bread and calls it His soma, His body, given for them, and the cup His haima, His blood, of the diatheke, the covenant, poured out for many. The words reach back to the blood of the Passover lamb in Egypt, to the covenant blood sprinkled at Sinai, and to the new covenant Jeremiah promised, in which God writes His law on hearts and remembers sins no more. In a few words over ordinary bread and wine, Jesus tells His disciples what His death will accomplish: a new covenant of forgiveness sealed in His own blood.

typology.fulfillment: Christ our Passover, sacrificed for us, whose blood delivers His people from death, Exodus 12:13 and 1 Corinthians 5:7.
Jesus' blood sealing the new covenant, fulfilling the covenant blood at Sinai and Jeremiah's promise, Exodus 24:8 and Jeremiah 31:31-34.
Jesus smitten so the sheep scatter, then gathered again after His resurrection, Zechariah 13:7 and Mark 14:28.

study.christ: Jesus moves toward the cross with open eyes and settled purpose. He receives a woman's costly devotion as an anointing for His burial, knowing what lies ahead. He is the true Passover lamb, and at the supper He unveils the meaning of His death before it comes: His body given, His blood poured out for many, the blood of a new covenant. He is betrayed by a friend and about to be deserted by all, yet He goes as it is written, laying down His life for the very disciples who will fail Him. In the bread and the cup He leaves His church a lasting sign of the sacrifice by which He saves.
study.god: God's saving plan unfolds precisely as the Scriptures foretold; the Son of Man goes as it is written, and even the betrayal and desertion serve God's purpose without lessening the guilt of those who sin. God is establishing a new covenant through the blood of His Son, fulfilling the promise to forgive His people's sins and write His law on their hearts. And God receives the extravagant love of a woman the world called wasteful, ensuring her deed is told wherever the gospel goes. This is a God who saves through sacrifice and treasures wholehearted devotion.

crossReferences.note: The blood of the lamb marking the homes God passes over, fulfilled in Christ our Passover.
Moses sprinkling the blood of the covenant, the words Jesus takes up over the cup.
The new covenant with sins forgiven, sealed in Jesus' blood at the Supper.
Paul hands on the institution of the Lord's Supper, to proclaim the Lord's death until He comes.

study.oneStory: The Passover that once rescued Israel from Egypt reaches its true meaning as Jesus, the Lamb of God, prepares to give His body and blood for many. In a borrowed upper room He seals the new covenant the prophets had promised, in which sins are forgiven forever and God's people are bound to Him. The lavish love of one woman and the coming failure of all the disciples frame the meal, making plain that the sacrifice Jesus is about to offer is for sinners, for betrayers and deserters, for us. The cross is now only hours away, and Jesus walks toward it as the willing, covenant-making Lamb.

quiz.q: How did Jesus interpret the woman's anointing at Bethany?

options.text: As a beautiful deed anointing His body in advance for burial, to be told wherever the gospel is preached.
options.note: Mark 14:8-9; Jesus honours her devotion and ties it to His coming death.

options.text: As a kind gesture that was, however, somewhat wasteful.
options.note: Others called it waste; Jesus called it beautiful and meaningful, rebuking their criticism.

options.text: As an act of love that pointed to His burial.
options.note: True, but it should include Jesus' promise that her deed would be told wherever the gospel goes.

options.text: As a distraction from caring for the poor.
options.note: Jesus defended her against exactly that charge, affirming her act toward Him.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say the bread and the cup represented at the Last Supper?

options.text: His body given and His blood of the covenant poured out for many.
options.note: Mark 14:22-24; the Supper signifies His sacrificial death sealing a new covenant.

options.text: A memorial of the exodus from Egypt only.
options.note: He transformed the Passover to point to His own body and blood, not merely the past exodus.

options.text: His sacrifice that inaugurates a new covenant of forgiveness.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should name specifically His body given and His blood poured out for many.

options.text: Ordinary food with no special significance.
options.note: Jesus gave the bread and cup profound meaning as His body and covenant blood.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Jesus setting His death at Passover?

options.text: He is the true Passover lamb whose blood delivers His people, as the lamb's blood spared Israel in Egypt.
options.note: The timing and words identify Jesus as the fulfilment of the Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

options.text: It was simply the season when He happened to be in Jerusalem.
options.note: Jesus deliberately tied His death to Passover to reveal its meaning.

options.text: It connects His death to the feast of Israel's redemption.
options.note: True, but it should name Jesus specifically as the true Passover lamb whose blood delivers.

options.text: It shows He wanted to reform the Passover ritual.
options.note: He fulfilled and reinterpreted the Passover around Himself, not merely reformed a ritual.

quiz.q: How does the anointing relate to Judas's betrayal in Mark's account?

options.text: Mark frames the woman's costly devotion between the plot and the betrayal, contrasting love with treachery.
options.note: The sandwich structure sets her beautiful deed against the surrounding scheming.

options.text: They are unrelated events told one after another.
options.note: Mark deliberately intertwines them to contrast devotion and betrayal.

options.text: Both show different responses people had to Jesus.
options.note: True, but it should note the deliberate contrast of extravagant love against betrayal.

options.text: Both show that following Jesus is about money.
options.note: The contrast concerns devotion versus greed, not that discipleship is about money.

quiz.q: What did Jesus foretell about His disciples that night?

options.text: That all would fall away and Peter would deny Him three times before the rooster crowed twice.
options.note: Mark 14:27-30; He foretold both the general desertion and Peter's specific denial.

options.text: That Peter would deny Him three times that night.
options.note: True, but it omits that all the disciples would fall away, fulfilling Zechariah's struck Shepherd.

options.text: That one disciple would betray Him but the rest stay faithful.
options.note: Jesus said all would fall away, not only that one would betray Him.

options.text: That the disciples would boldly defend Him to the end.
options.note: The opposite; He foretold they would all desert Him.

questions: The woman gave something extravagantly costly to Jesus, and others called it waste. What would wholehearted, even lavish, devotion to Christ look like for you?
Judas was one of the Twelve, close to Jesus, yet betrayed Him. How does his example warn you against nearness to Jesus without a true heart?
Jesus gave His body and blood for many, including disciples who would fail Him. How does it move you that He died knowing you too would sometimes fail Him?
Every time the church shares the bread and cup, it proclaims the Lord's death. How can you come to the Lord's Supper with deeper understanding and gratitude?
Peter was confident he would never fall, yet he did. Where might overconfidence in your own faithfulness be leaving you unguarded?

translationNotes.note: My blood of the covenant echoes Exodus 24:8; some manuscripts read the new covenant, making explicit the fulfilment of Jeremiah 31:31. Poured out for many recalls the servant of Isaiah 53 who bears the sin of many.

The phrase identifying the betrayer as one of the Twelve who dips in the bowl originally used a dash; rendered here with a comma. The shared bowl heightens the horror of betrayal by a table companion.

I will strike the Shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered quotes Zechariah 13:7; the scattering of the disciples fulfils prophecy, and Jesus at once promises to gather them again in Galilee after He rises.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 14:1-31. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 20 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
